chapters.title: The eternal battle of "Qs"
chapters.text:
American businessman William E. Conway treats productivity numbers the way others treat accident statistics. They tell you something is wrong, but they do not stop the damage.

In American business culture, quality and output are often seen as rivals. Pick one, the thinking goes, and you sacrifice the other. Many plant managers have watched this play out. Push for quality and production drops. Push for output and quality drops.

Real change only comes through a long-term commitment to learning.

Executives at many firms are trying to fix what ails American industry. There has been progress. Lower costs, stronger competitive positions, and happier employees are slowly becoming the norm.

The secret to a sustainable business is appealing, and we will get to it soon.

chapters.title: The Japanese scenario for the American case
chapters.text:
In 1950, Japan had almost no natural resources. Oil, coal, iron ore, copper, manganese, even timber were in short supply. Yet Japan built a strong reputation for affordable, well-made consumer goods. The country still has to export goods in order to import food and equipment, and reaching that level of excellence was a heavy lift for senior management. They made it work.

Western industries, by contrast, struggle with several issues. The most common problems are weak commitment to developing marketable products and services, an obsession with short-term numbers, infrequent performance reviews and merit ratings, high management turnover, overreliance on the numbers that are easy to measure while ignoring the rest, and uncontrolled medical costs.

To reverse the slide of Western industry, management style has to change. The first step is admitting the problem. The second is acting on it. That responsibility belongs to management.

There is now a working theory for raising quality, productivity, and competitiveness. It pushes back against the idea that there is nothing new to learn about management. It also gives business school students a benchmark for judging their curriculum. Are schools addressing today's problems, or are they quietly going out of date? Outdated does not have to be intentional. It can creep in unnoticed.

Short-term profit is a poor measure of how good a manager really is.

A company can keep paying dividends by skipping maintenance, cutting research, or buying another business, but those moves only work for a while. Senior management cannot just sign off on quality and productivity for their own tenure. They have to understand their commitments and follow through on them. Management should not pass the work down the chain. They have to lead it directly, not just offer support from the corner office.

chapters.title: The 14 points of management
chapters.text:
The 14 points are the foundation for transforming an industry. They were first observed in Japan and later showed up in the United States. When a company adopts them, it signals that management is committed to staying in business and protecting both shareholders and employees.

Hard work alone does not lift productivity. Smart work matters too.

The 14 points of management are as follows. First, create a clear purpose for improving products and services so the company becomes more competitive, stays in business, and keeps creating jobs. Second, adopt a new mindset. Western management has to see the threats, understand its duties, and step into leadership. Third, build quality into the product from the start so a massive inspection at the end is not needed.

Fourth, stop awarding contracts based on price alone. Instead, work to minimize total cost by building long-term relationships with a small number of suppliers, grounded in loyalty and trust. Fifth, keep improving the manufacturing and service system so quality rises, productivity rises, and costs fall. Sixth, run on-the-job training programs.

Seventh, install real leadership. Supervision should help people and machines perform better. Eighth, build a workplace without fear, where everyone can contribute to the company's success. Ninth, tear down the walls between departments so research, design, sales, and production can work together. That teamwork lets the company solve product and service problems before they grow.

Tenth, drop the slogans, pep talks, and arbitrary targets like zero defects or productivity jumps. These usually damage relationships, because the real causes of poor quality and low productivity tend to be systemic and outside any worker's control. Eleventh, remove quotas on the shop floor and replace them with leadership, because managing by the numbers alone is not enough.

Twelfth, remove the barriers that keep workers, engineers, and managers from taking pride in their work. Thirteenth, offer a comprehensive program of education and self-improvement. Fourteenth, bring everyone into the transition. The change belongs to all of them.

chapters.title: The consumer-company tandem in action
chapters.text:
Consumers often need years before they can give a fair judgment of a product or service. A car buyer, for instance, will have a more useful view of a vehicle a year after the purchase than on the day they drive it off the lot. For plant managers, quality usually means meeting specifications and following procedures. Their job also includes constant process improvement and leadership development, whether they realize it or not.

The exchange between consumers and companies is critical to product design and service delivery. This exchange, sometimes called democracy in industry, often leads to more cost-effective offerings that fit customer needs better. The wider public also plays a part through word of mouth between manufacturers, current users, and future buyers.

Listening to what customers need often pushes a product further than advertising alone.

That said, consumers are not great at predicting which new products or services will succeed in the years ahead. Successful offerings usually come from the producer's knowledge, creativity, innovation, and willingness to take risks. Building them requires experimentation and steady funding to weather the slow first months on the market.

Leaders play a central role in strengthening the system, which lets everyone do better work and find more satisfaction in it. That role also includes training new employees so they can perform well from day one.

Good leadership reaches beyond the company. It boosts productivity and draws useful feedback from customers. That back-and-forth between consumer input and company innovation is what drives long-term success in the market.

According to the Economic Policy Institute, productivity and wages moved together from 1948 through 1979, growing 108 percent and 93 percent. From 1979 on, productivity rose 70 percent while wages rose only 12 percent.

chapters.title: Consistency is the path to standards
chapters.text:
The meaning of clean changes with context, from restaurant dishes to computer hard drives. People in business and government need a deep understanding of how products perform, how drugs work, and how human effort adds up. That understanding is often missing from management and science literature, even though it is essential for tackling industrial problems.

Meaning starts as an idea in someone's mind. Any term, rule, instruction, or specification carries only one communicative meaning, which is the record of what happens when a particular action or test is performed. That is what an operational definition looks like. Words like good, dependable, uniform, or safe carry no useful meaning until they are spelled out in operational terms, with samples, tests, and criteria.

An operational definition is one that people can actually use. Terms like safe, round, and reliable have to be spelled out clearly and understood in the same way by vendors, buyers, and production workers, both today and down the road.

A rule can only have legal force when it is clear how to apply it.

Allowing repeated violations without consequence invites chaos and erodes public trust. Rules must be strict by design. Citing industry standards inside regulations also makes those regulations more useful and more meaningful.

For example, photographers and engineers immediately recognize a focal length-to-diameter ratio like 2.8 for a lens. Replacing a dead AA battery with one bought anywhere in the world is easy, even if the quality varies by brand. The convenience of a 110-volt standard and matching outlets across most of the Northern Hemisphere is hard to overstate.

As W. Edwards Deming wrote, practice is more exacting than pure science, and more exacting than teaching.

chapters.title: Quality always has the last laugh
chapters.text:
Management has to understand process capability, the capability of measurement systems, and the need for statistical control when using instruments and gauges. They also have to recognize that adjusting instruments to a standard should rest on statistical evidence, not on gut feel. At the same time, management has to understand that singling out workers for low output or high error rates is wrong, inefficient, and costly.

A reliable rule of thumb is that better-quality products earn more profit, even when production costs stay flat.

Supervisors often make the mistake of over-correcting when they call attention to an error before confirming who caused it. When a company adopts the 14 points and clears out the major obstacles, progress usually follows the same upward path it took in the early months, and can keep climbing for up to two years. The big difference is that quality and productivity do not level off under a good plan. They keep rising as long as management stays in charge of the program.

Defects still happen on the line. A perfect item can be installed wrong, or two wires can be crossed. Products can also be damaged in shipping. None of these problems are fun, but stewing over them does not help. Treating each mistake as a lesson does.

Knowledge is one of a company's most valuable assets. Unlike physical resources, knowledge grows with use. Formal education and self-study both make a worker more valuable. If you want to raise the bar, put money and effort into training your people.

As W. Edwards Deming once said, if you control an industry's standards, you control that industry lock, stock, and ledger.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
A quality improvement program helps anyone who produces a product, delivers a service, or runs research. It raises the quality of the work while lifting production with less effort at lower cost. Service quality needs the same attention as manufacturing quality. The job of leadership is to help people and machines perform better, lift quality, raise output, and build pride.

Leadership is hard work. If none of this scares you, you may be a natural captain, and it may be time to step up and pave the way.

A practical step is this. If you are responsible for a production or service facility, try these habits to lift productivity and quality. Look closely at your current workflow. Update your procedures and technology. Commit to routine maintenance. Train and educate your team thoroughly. Keep the work area organized. Hold a healthy level of inventory.
